mappings.rationaleAI: Directly traces the history of epidemic disease and evolving theories of disease causation from ancient humoral medicine through germ theory, covering plague, smallpox, and the demographic consequences of infectious disease.

Examines how pathogens — bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites — emerge through zoonotic spillover from animal reservoirs, and how genomic techniques trace pathogen evolutionary histories to inform surveillance and pandemic prevention.

Fleming's discovery and Florey and Chain's mass production of penicillin as the first major antibiotic transformed the treatment of bacterial infections, while the growing crisis of antimicrobial resistance raises ongoing challenges for pharmacodynamic and chemotherapeutic approaches.

Bacteriophages as viruses that destroy bacteria offer a biological therapy alternative to antibiotics for treating chronic bacterial infections, directly relevant to managing infectious disease amid growing antimicrobial resistance.

Pasteur and Koch's germ theory established the microbial causation of infectious disease, enabling breakthroughs in antibiotics, vaccines, and sterilization foundational to understanding disease causes and contagious illness.

Covers hormonal roles in homeostasis and the consequences of hormonal imbalance for health, directly relevant to endocrine system disorders of the pituitary, thyroid, adrenals, gonads, and pancreas, as well as metabolic diseases.
Mutations as molecular mechanisms producing cancers, hereditary diseases, and contributing to aging directly address disease causation, tumours, and congenital disorders present at birth.

Free radicals as agents of radiation damage causing cumulative cellular harm linked to aging and cancer, alongside their aberrant role in cell division, connect to disorders caused by physical agents and tumour formation.

The Curie family's radioactivity research led directly to radiation-based cancer treatment, and synthesis of artificial radioactive isotopes expanded medical applications of radiation therapy for tumours.

Hysteria as a diagnosed condition involving conversion of emotional distress into physical symptoms spans historical disease classification from Hippocratic theory through Freud's psychoanalytic reinterpretation, relevant to disorders of the central nervous system and evolving diagnostic practice.

Burton's encyclopaedic synthesis of melancholy as a disease caused by humoral imbalance affecting mind and body anticipates modern conceptions of depression, connecting historical disease classification with central nervous system disorders.

Humoral medicine's framework of disease as imbalance among four bodily fluids dominated Western medical thought for two millennia, directly shaping theories of disease causation and therapeutic practices including bloodletting, purging, and dietary regulation.

Techniques for manipulating genetic material have direct medical applications in treating hereditary and congenital conditions, representing a form of biological therapy with transformative potential for diseases present at birth.

Neuroimaging advances that reveal the active brain in real time are directly relevant to detecting and diagnosing neurological and psychiatric conditions, with implications for treating disorders of the central nervous system.

Cholera epidemics from waterborne pathogens in the contaminated Thames and Bazalgette's sewer engineering as a public health intervention illustrate the transmission and prevention of infectious contagious disease.

Mitochondrial dysfunction is linked to neurodegenerative diseases and aging, and mitochondria are central to biomedical research into metabolic disorders, connecting cellular energetics to specific disease categories.
